sentiment.rationale: Job is introduced as a righteous man, but Satan afflicts him with the loss of his children and possessions, leading to deep grief.
Satan smites Job with boils; his wife urges him to curse God, yet Job refuses. His three friends arrive and sit in silence at his suffering.
Job curses the day of his birth in anguished poetry, wishing he had never been born or had died at birth.
Eliphaz begins his first speech, arguing that the innocent do not perish and implying Job must have sinned.
Eliphaz continues, urging Job to seek God and accept His correction, promising restoration to the faithful.
Job responds with deep anguish, lamenting that his grief outweighs the sand of the sea and his friends have failed him.
Job compares life to hard service and questions why God targets him with suffering rather than leaving him alone.
Bildad insists God does not pervert justice and tells Job to repent, citing the fate of the wicked.
Job acknowledges God's overwhelming power but despairs that no mortal can contend with or answer God in court.
Job pours out his complaint to God, asking why He formed him only to destroy him and wishing for death.
Zophar rebukes Job sharply, insisting God exacts less than Job's iniquity deserves, and calls on him to repent.
Job responds with sarcasm and then describes God's sovereign, inscrutable power over nations and nature.
Job rebukes his friends as worthless physicians and boldly declares he will argue his case directly before God.
Job reflects on the brevity and frailty of human life, longing for hope beyond the grave.
Eliphaz's second speech harshly accuses Job of undermining religion and describes the fate of the wicked.
Job calls his friends miserable comforters and describes God's assault on him in vivid, agonizing imagery.
Job's spirit is broken; he sees the grave as his house and finds no hope among his companions.
Bildad's second speech describes the terrible fate of the wicked in graphic detail, implicitly aimed at Job.
Job feels persecuted by both God and friends but rises to declare his Redeemer lives and he shall see God in the flesh.
Zophar's second speech describes how the wicked will be punished, losing all their ill-gotten gains.
Job challenges the friends' doctrine by observing that the wicked often prosper, contradicting their theology.
Eliphaz's third speech accuses Job of specific sins including oppressing the poor, and urges him to return to God.
Job yearns to find God and present his case, confident he would be acquitted, but God remains hidden.
Job catalogs injustices in the world — oppression of the poor, theft, murder — questioning why God does not intervene.
Bildad's brief third speech declares God's dominion and argues that no mortal can be righteous before Him.
Job describes God's majestic power over creation — hanging the earth on nothing, commanding the sea, adorning the heavens.
Job maintains his integrity with an oath and describes the ultimate fate of the wicked and the hypocrite.
A magnificent wisdom poem: humanity mines earth's treasures but true wisdom is found only through the fear of the Lord.
Job nostalgically recalls his former days of honor, prosperity, and community respect when God watched over him.
Job contrasts his present misery with his former glory — mocked by the lowest of society, afflicted and abandoned.
Job's great oath of innocence: he systematically denies every possible sin and invites God's judgment if any charge is true.
Young Elihu, angry at both Job and his friends, begins his speeches, claiming the Spirit gives him understanding.
Elihu argues God speaks through dreams and suffering to turn people from pride, and describes a mediating angel.
Elihu defends God's justice, insisting God does not pervert right and governs all nations with perfect equity.
Elihu argues that human righteousness or wickedness does not affect God but affects other humans.
Elihu extols God's justice and mercy, warning the afflicted to learn from discipline, then transitions to God's majesty in nature.
Elihu describes God's awesome power in thunderstorms, snow, ice, and wind, urging reverent awe before the Almighty.
God speaks from the whirlwind, challenging Job with questions about creation — the earth's foundations, the sea, light, and the stars.
God continues questioning Job about the wild animals He sustains — goats, donkeys, oxen, ostriches, horses, and hawks.
God challenges Job to respond; Job puts his hand over his mouth. God then describes the mighty Behemoth.
God describes the fearsome Leviathan in elaborate detail, demonstrating His sovereign power over all creation.
Job repents in dust and ashes, God rebukes the friends, and Job is restored with double his former blessings.

summaries.summary: Job is introduced as a blameless and upright man; Satan challenges God that Job's righteousness is due to his blessings, and God permits Satan to destroy Job's possessions and children.
Satan smites Job with painful boils from head to foot; his wife tells him to curse God and die, but Job refuses. Three friends arrive and sit in silence for seven days.
Job breaks his silence and curses the day of his birth, wishing he had never been born or had died at birth, longing for the rest of death.
Eliphaz begins the first cycle of debate, appealing to a mysterious night vision and arguing that the innocent do not perish — only the wicked suffer.
Eliphaz continues, urging Job to seek God and accept His chastening, promising that God wounds but also heals and that restoration awaits the penitent.
Job responds with deep anguish, saying his grief is heavier than the sand of the sea, and accuses his friends of being unreliable like seasonal streams.
Job compares human life to hard service and a weaver's shuttle, then turns his complaint directly to God, asking why He will not leave him alone.
Bildad enters the debate, insisting God does not pervert justice, suggesting Job's children died for their own sin, and urging Job to seek God for restoration.
Job acknowledges God's overwhelming wisdom and power but despairs that no mortal can contend with God or find a fair hearing in His court.
Job pours out his complaint to God, questioning why God formed him only to crush him, and pleads for a brief respite before death.
Zophar sharply rebukes Job, insisting that God is exacting less than Job deserves, and calls on Job to put away iniquity and find peace.
Job responds sarcastically that his friends are not the only wise men, then describes God's inscrutable sovereign power that overturns nations and counselors.
Job rebukes his friends as worthless physicians who speak falsely for God, then boldly declares he will argue his case directly before the Almighty.
Job reflects on the brevity and frailty of human life, comparing man to a flower that fades, and longs for hope of renewal beyond the grave.
Eliphaz's second speech accuses Job of undermining piety with his words and paints a vivid picture of the restless torment of the wicked.
Job calls his friends miserable comforters and describes God's assault on him in vivid imagery — torn, shaken, shattered — yet appeals to his witness in heaven.
Job's spirit is broken and his hope is gone; he sees the grave as his only house and finds no understanding among his companions.
Bildad's second speech describes the fate of the wicked in terrifying detail — snares, disease, death, and being forgotten from the earth.
Job cries out against his friends' cruelty and God's persecution, but rises to a climactic declaration that his Redeemer lives and he shall see God in the flesh.
Zophar's second speech argues that the triumph of the wicked is short-lived and God will force them to disgorge their ill-gotten wealth.
Job challenges his friends' theology by pointing out that the wicked often live long, prosper, and die in ease, contradicting their doctrine of retribution.
Eliphaz's third speech accuses Job of specific sins — stripping the naked, withholding water, oppressing widows — and urges him to return to God for restoration.
Job yearns to find God and lay his case before Him, confident he would be acquitted, but God is hidden and Job trembles before His inscrutable will.
Job catalogs the injustices of the world — the wicked oppress the poor, steal, murder — and questions why God does not set times of judgment.
Bildad's brief third speech declares God's dominion and purity, arguing that even the moon and stars are not pure in His sight, much less mortal man.
Job describes God's majestic power over creation — the shades tremble beneath the waters, He stretches the north over the void and hangs the earth on nothing.
Job swears by the living God that he will maintain his integrity and never admit the friends are right, then describes the hopelessness of the wicked.
A magnificent wisdom poem: humanity mines precious stones from the earth's depths, but true wisdom cannot be bought — it is found only through the fear of the Lord.
Job nostalgically recalls his former days of honor and prosperity, when the elders rose in his presence and the blessing of the dying rested on him.
Job contrasts his former glory with present misery — mocked by the lowest of society, afflicted in body, crying out to God with no answer.
Job's great oath of innocence: he systematically denies every possible sin — lust, falsehood, oppression, greed, idolatry — and invites God's judgment if guilty.
Young Elihu, angry that the three friends could not answer Job, and that Job justified himself rather than God, begins his speeches with passionate urgency.
Elihu argues that God speaks to humanity through dreams and suffering to preserve their souls, and describes an angelic mediator who can ransom the afflicted.
Elihu defends God's absolute justice, insisting that the Almighty cannot do wickedness and governs all creation with perfect equity and impartiality.
Elihu argues that human righteousness or wickedness does not profit or diminish God — it affects other mortals — and that the afflicted must wait on God.
Elihu extols God's justice toward the afflicted, warning that discipline is meant to open ears to instruction, then turns to praise God's greatness in nature.
Elihu describes God's awesome power displayed in thunder, lightning, snow, and ice, concluding that the Almighty is beyond human understanding and worthy of reverent fear.
God answers Job from the whirlwind with a series of unanswerable questions about creation — the earth's foundations, the sea, dawn, snow, stars, and storms.
God continues questioning Job about the wild animals He governs — mountain goats, wild donkeys, the wild ox, the ostrich, the horse, the hawk, and the eagle.
God challenges Job to respond; Job humbly covers his mouth. God then describes the mighty Behemoth, demonstrating His power over the greatest of land creatures.
God describes the fearsome Leviathan in elaborate detail — its impenetrable armor, fiery breath, and unconquerable strength — showing none can stand before its Maker.
Job repents in dust and ashes, God rebukes the three friends for not speaking rightly, and Job is restored with twice his former possessions and ten new children.

crossReferences.note: We brought nothing into this world and can carry nothing out.
Naked came he and naked shall he return.
Paul quotes this verse about God taking the wise in their own craftiness.
The question of whether man shall live again, answered by Christ's resurrection.
Job's declaration of a living Redeemer parallels Isaiah's proclamation of the Lord as Redeemer.
Seeing God in the flesh anticipates the doctrine of bodily resurrection.
God stretches the north over the empty place and hangs the earth upon nothing.
The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom.
God laying the foundations of the earth, paralleling wisdom's role at creation.
The morning stars singing together at creation echoes the premortal council.
Moving from hearing to seeing God parallels Moses' direct experience of God's presence.
James cites the patience of Job and the Lord's compassionate outcome.

verses.reason: 'The LORD gave, and the LORD hath taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD' — one of the most quoted verses on suffering.
Job's response to his wife: 'shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil?'
'Let the day perish wherein I was born' — the iconic opening of Job's lament.
Eliphaz's core argument: 'who ever perished, being innocent?'
'Happy is the man whom God correcteth' — the doctrine of divine chastening.
Job's grief is heavier than the sand of the sea — a vivid image of overwhelming sorrow.
'What is man, that thou shouldest magnify him?' — a bitter counterpoint to Psalm 8.
Job longs for a 'daysman' (mediator) to arbitrate between him and God — foreshadowing Christ.
'Ask now the beasts, and they shall teach thee' — nature testifies of God's hand.
'Though he slay me, yet will I trust in him' — a defining statement of faith under trial.
'If a man die, shall he live again?' — the great question of resurrection.
'My witness is in heaven, and my record is on high' — Job's appeal to a heavenly advocate.
'I know that my redeemer liveth' — one of the most powerful messianic declarations in the Old Testament.
'In my flesh shall I see God' — a profound statement of belief in bodily resurrection.
'Acquaint now thyself with him, and be at peace' — Eliphaz's appeal, ironic given his false accusations.
'When he hath tried me, I shall come forth as gold' — faith refined through testing.
'He hangeth the earth upon nothing' — a remarkable cosmological statement for the ancient world.
'Till I die I will not remove mine integrity from me' — Job's unwavering moral resolve.
'The fear of the Lord, that is wisdom; and to depart from evil is understanding' — the poem's climactic conclusion.
'I made a covenant with mine eyes' — Job's commitment to moral purity.
'A messenger, an interpreter, one among a thousand' — Elihu's description of a divine mediator.
'Stand still, and consider the wondrous works of God' — a call to contemplative worship.
'Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth?' — God's opening challenge from the whirlwind.
'When the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy' — the premortal celebration.
'Behold, I am vile; what shall I answer thee? I will lay mine hand upon my mouth' — Job's first humble response.
'I have heard of thee by the hearing of the ear: but now mine eye seeth thee' — Job's transformation from theology to theophany.
'The LORD turned the captivity of Job, when he prayed for his friends' — restoration through intercession.
'The LORD blessed the latter end of Job more than his beginning' — double restoration.
